It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The click had no impact - board didn't change. This means clicking (36, 40) didn't place pink. Maybe the gray slot's center isn't at (36, 40). Let me find the exact position of the empty gray slot by looking at segmentation more carefully.
Also, I notice the bottom panel shows pink selected. But clicking the gray slot didn't work. Maybe I need to click on the actual gray pixel, not the center.
Let me inspect the segmentation to find the exact boundary of the empty gray slot.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click had no impact. Need to find exact position of empty gray slot
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

for i, node in enumerate(seg):
    if node['color'] == 'g' and node['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"Node {i}: color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
        b = node['boundary']
        if b:
            min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
            max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
            min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
            max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
            center_r = (min_r + max_r) // 2
            center_c = (min_c + max_c) // 2
            print(f"  Bbox: ({min_r},{min_c})-({max_r},{max_c}), center=({center_r},{center_c})")

# Also check what's in the green container area
print("\nAll nodes in green container region (rows 30-40):")
for i, node in enumerate(seg):
    b = node['boundary']
    if b:
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        if 30 <= min_r <= 40:
            print(f"  Node {i}: color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The empty gray slot is at boundary [[36, 40], [36, 41], [37, 41], [37, 40]] with center (36, 40). I clicked (36, 40) but it had no impact. 
Wait - looking at the green container contents:
- Node 28: blue at (35,21)-(38,24) - 4x4 block
- Node 29: light green (N) at (35,27)-(38,30) - 4x4 block
- Node 30: yellow at (35,33)-(38,36) - 4x4 block
- Node 31: gray at (36,40)-(37,41) - 2x2 slot
The filled slots are 4x4 blocks (16 pixels) while the empty slot is 2x2 (4 pixels). This is the same pattern as before - empty slots are 2x2 gray squares, filled slots are 4x4 colored squares.
But clicking (36, 40) had no impact! Maybe the issue is that pink is already selected, and clicking the slot should place it. But it didn't work.
Wait - let me look at the bottom panel. The bottom shows gray squares and one pink square. The pink is selected. But maybe I need to click on the pink square first to "pick it up" and then click the slot?
Actually, looking at the previous successful placements, I clicked the color first, then the slot. Let me check if pink is actually selected or not. The image shows pink with a white border at the bottom, suggesting it IS selected.
Hmm, but clicking (36, 40) had no impact. Let me try clicking on a different position within the 2x2 gray area. The boundary is [[36, 40], [36, 41], [37, 41], [37, 40]], so the cells are (36,40), (36,41), (37,40), (37,41). I clicked (36, 40). Let me try (37, 41) or the center of the 2x2 block.
Actually wait - maybe the issue is that the pink color square at the bottom is NOT selected. Let me look at the bottom panel more carefully. The bottom shows: gray, gray, gray, gray, pink, gray, gray. The pink one appears to have a white border, meaning it's selected.
Let me try clicking the pink square at the bottom first, then the gray slot.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Pink appears selected but clicking slot had no impact
# Let me try: click pink first to select, then click the slot

# Find pink at bottom
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
pink_pos = None
for i, node in enumerate(seg):
    if node['color'] == 'M' and node['pixels'] == 16:
        b = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        if min_r >= 54:
            center_r = (min(p[0] for p in b) + max(p[0] for p in b)) // 2
            center_c = (min(p[1] for p in b) + max(p[1] for p in b)) // 2
            pink_pos = (center_r, center_c)
            break

print(f"Pink position: {pink_pos}")

# Try selecting pink then clicking slot
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': pink_pos[0], 'col': pink_pos[1]},
         {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 40}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
